title: The habit that changed my year
summary: Narrate a small daily habit you kept for at least six months and explain what changed as a result, using past simple, present perfect, and a conditional.
topic: A small daily habit that compounded over time
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (400–500 words) narrating a small daily habit you started and kept for at least six months. Describe when and how you started it, what it involved each day, and what you noticed had changed after several months. Use past simple for events, present perfect for ongoing results, and include a conditional sentence about what might have happened if you had stopped early.
hintStarters: I started the habit almost by accident, when…
After about three months, I began to notice that…
If I had given up after the first week, I would never have discovered…

hintVocab.meaningVi: hầu như không nhận thấy được
một quyết định có ý thức
thực hiện một thay đổi nhỏ

hintVocab.phrase: to carry something into the day
hintVocab.meaningVi: mang điều gì đó theo suốt ngày

sampleText:
I started keeping a handwritten log almost by accident. In March last year I was living in a rented room in Tbilisi, working remotely, and I noticed that my days were blurring together — I could not remember what I had done on Tuesday versus Thursday. On a whim, I bought a small notebook at a stationery shop near the Dry Bridge market and started writing three sentences every evening before bed: what I did, what I ate, and one thing that surprised me. The entries took about four minutes. It felt like a very small commitment, and I had broken larger ones before. The smallness was the point.

For the first month, nothing seemed to change. The notebook sat on my desk and I filled it in the way you might fill in a form — dutifully, without much feeling. Around week six, something shifted. I started to notice that the act of naming a surprise — even something small, like a stranger asking me for directions in Georgian, or a cloud formation that stopped me on the street — was changing the way I moved through the day. I was paying closer attention to things because I knew I would have to write something down later. By the end of three months, I had logged ninety-two consecutive days, and the log had become a record I genuinely wanted to re-read. I started going back to entries from a month earlier, surprised by what I had forgotten and surprised again by what had quietly accumulated without my noticing. The log was becoming something I had not set out to make.

By October, I had filled three notebooks. I have not stopped since. The habit has made the year feel longer in the best way — richer and more differentiated, with fewer stretches that simply disappeared. If I had given up after that first blank, dutiful month, I would never have discovered what the habit was actually for: not documentation, but attention. The four minutes at the end of the day were not the point; the point was the small, accumulated alertness that carried through every hour before them. I did not plan for that. The habit taught me what it was doing while I was doing it. That seems to be how the most useful habits work: quietly, and not quite in the way you expected. I started the habit with no theory and no system. Three notebooks later, I would not stop it for anything.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Third conditional to reflect on a past choice
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'If I had given up after that first blank, dutiful month, I would never have discovered what the habit was actually for' dùng điều kiện loại 3 (if + had + quá khứ phân từ, would have + quá khứ phân từ) để suy ngẫm về điều gì có thể đã xảy ra nếu một quyết định trong quá khứ khác đi.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'If I had given up after that first blank, dutiful month, I would never have discovered what the habit was actually for' uses a third conditional (if + had + past participle, would have + past participle) to reflect on what might have happened if a past decision had been different.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect to describe an ongoing result
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'The habit has made the year feel longer in the best way' dùng hiện tại hoàn thành vì hành động (thói quen được duy trì) đã bắt đầu trong quá khứ nhưng kết quả — cảm giác năm dài hơn — vẫn còn hiệu lực đến hiện tại.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'The habit has made the year feel longer in the best way' uses the present perfect because the action (keeping the habit) started in the past and its result — the year feeling richer — is still present now.

sampleTranslationVi:
Tôi bắt đầu ghi nhật ký bằng tay gần như một cách tình cờ. Vào tháng Ba năm ngoái, tôi đang sống trong một phòng thuê ở Tbilisi, làm việc từ xa, và tôi nhận thấy rằng các ngày của tôi đang trộn lẫn vào nhau — tôi không thể nhớ mình đã làm gì vào thứ Ba so với thứ Năm. Một cách ngẫu hứng, tôi mua một cuốn sổ nhỏ tại một cửa hàng văn phòng phẩm gần chợ Cầu Cạn và bắt đầu viết ba câu mỗi tối trước khi đi ngủ: tôi đã làm gì, tôi đã ăn gì, và một điều khiến tôi ngạc nhiên. Mỗi mục tốn khoảng bốn phút.

Trong tháng đầu tiên, dường như không có gì thay đổi. Cuốn sổ nằm trên bàn tôi và tôi điền vào đó theo cách bạn có thể điền vào một biểu mẫu — một cách chu đáo, không mấy cảm xúc. Khoảng tuần thứ sáu, có gì đó đã thay đổi. Tôi bắt đầu nhận thấy rằng hành động đặt tên cho một điều bất ngờ — dù chỉ là điều nhỏ, như một người lạ hỏi tôi đường bằng tiếng Gruzia, hay một đám mây khiến tôi dừng lại trên đường — đang thay đổi cách tôi đi qua ngày. Tôi đang chú ý đến mọi thứ kỹ hơn vì tôi biết rằng mình sẽ phải viết gì đó xuống sau. Vào cuối ba tháng, tôi đã ghi chép liên tiếp chín mươi hai ngày, và cuốn nhật ký đã trở thành một hồ sơ mà tôi thực sự muốn đọc lại.

Vào tháng Mười, tôi đã lấp đầy ba cuốn sổ. Tôi đã không dừng lại kể từ đó. Thói quen này đã khiến năm cảm thấy dài hơn theo cách tốt nhất — phong phú hơn và có nhiều sự khác biệt hơn, với ít khoảng thời gian hơn đơn giản là biến mất. Nếu tôi đã từ bỏ sau tháng đầu tiên nhạt nhẽo, mang tính bổn phận đó, tôi sẽ không bao giờ khám phá ra thói quen thực sự để làm gì: không phải ghi chép, mà là sự chú ý. Bốn phút vào cuối ngày không phải là điểm quan trọng; điểm quan trọng là sự tỉnh thức nhỏ bé, được tích lũy kéo dài qua từng giờ trước đó. Tôi không lên kế hoạch cho điều đó. Thói quen đã dạy tôi nó đang làm gì trong khi tôi đang thực hiện nó.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a third conditional to reflect on a past choice?
options: If I had given up in week one, I would never have discovered the real benefit.
If I gave up in week one, I would never have discovered the real benefit.
If I had given up in week one, I would never discover the real benefit.
If I had given up in week one, I will never have discovered the real benefit.
mcQuestions.explanation: Third conditional: if + had + past participle, + would have + past participle. Using 'gave' makes it second conditional (unreal present). 'Would never discover' omits 'have'. 'Will' cannot appear in the main clause of a third conditional.
mcQuestions.hint: Third conditional = if + had + past participle, would + have + past participle.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the present perfect to describe an ongoing result?
options: The habit has made the year feel richer and more memorable.
The habit made the year feel richer and more memorable since March.
The habit has been making the year feel richer and more memorable since the start.
The habit have made the year feel richer and more memorable.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect (has + past participle) links a past cause to its present result. 'Made … since March' uses simple past incorrectly with 'since'. 'Has been making' is present perfect continuous, which stresses duration rather than result. 'Habit have' uses the wrong auxiliary for a singular subject.
mcQuestions.hint: For a completed cause with a present result, use present perfect (has + past participle).

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses past simple and past continuous together?
options: I noticed something had changed while I was writing in the notebook each evening.
I noticed something had changed while I wrote in the notebook each evening.
I noticed something changed while I was writing in the notebook each evening.
I was noticing something had changed while I was writing in the notebook each evening.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past perfect ('had changed') describes a change that happened before the noticing. Past continuous ('was writing') describes the background action. 'While I wrote' is less natural for an ongoing background. Option C changes the meaning by implying the change was simultaneous. Option D uses two continuous forms awkwardly.
mcQuestions.hint: Use past perfect for the earlier event and past continuous for the background action.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase most naturally describes days that lost their distinctiveness?
options: My days were blurring together.
My days were blending themselves together.
My days were confusing themselves together.
My days were mixing up together.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Days were blurring together' is the natural idiomatic expression. 'Blending themselves' and 'confusing themselves' are not standard. 'Mixing up together' is non-idiomatic — 'mix up' means to confuse two different things.
mcQuestions.hint: Which verb collocates naturally with days losing their distinctiveness?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'on a whim' correctly to describe a spontaneous decision?
options: On a whim, I bought a small notebook and started writing every evening.
On a whim of, I bought a small notebook and started writing every evening.
In a whim, I bought a small notebook and started writing every evening.
With a whim, I bought a small notebook and started writing every evening.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'On a whim' is the correct fixed expression. 'On a whim of', 'in a whim', and 'with a whim' are not standard forms of this idiom.
mcQuestions.hint: Only one preposition collocates with 'whim' in this fixed expression.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'began to' to mark the start of a gradual change?
options: Around week six, I began to notice that something had shifted.
Around week six, I began noticing that something shifted.
Around week six, I began to noticed that something had shifted.
Around week six, I was beginning noticing that something had shifted.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Began to + infinitive' is the standard form. 'Began to noticed' uses a past form after 'to'. 'Was beginning noticing' creates two gerunds awkwardly. Option B ('began noticing that something shifted') is grammatically possible but loses the past-perfect contrast.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Began to' is followed by the bare infinitive, not a past form.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'since' correctly with the present perfect?
options: I have not stopped writing since last March.
I have not stopped writing since last March ago.
I did not stop writing since last March.
I have not stopped writing from last March.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect + 'since' + a past time point is correct. 'Since … ago' is a redundant combination. 'Did not stop … since' mixes simple past with 'since'. 'From last March' is non-standard in this context — 'since' is the correct preposition.
mcQuestions.hint: With a starting time point, 'since' pairs with present perfect. 'Ago' is not needed after 'since'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence most naturally expresses that the real purpose was not what was expected?
options: The habit was not about documentation; it was about attention.
The habit was not about to documentation; it was about to attention.
The habit was not for documentation; it was for attention.
The habit was not in documentation; it was in attention.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'About' is the natural preposition when describing what a habit is fundamentally concerned with. 'About to' means 'on the point of doing something' — a different meaning. 'Not for … for' and 'not in … in' are unnatural in this context.
mcQuestions.hint: Which preposition naturally follows 'be' when describing what something is fundamentally concerned with?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'consecutive' to describe an unbroken sequence?
options: I logged ninety consecutive days without missing once.
I logged ninety consequent days without missing once.
I logged ninety consecutive days without miss once.
I logged ninety continuous days without missing once.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Consecutive' means following one after another without gaps. 'Consequent' means 'resulting from' — a different word. 'Without miss' needs a gerund ('missing'). 'Continuous' is technically possible but 'consecutive' is the standard word for counting unbroken days.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Consecutive' means unbroken days in sequence. Check the form after 'without'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'carry through' to describe something that persists?
options: That small alertness carried through every hour of the day.
That small alertness carried over every hour of the day.
That small alertness carried along every hour of the day.
That small alertness carried across every hour of the day.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Carry through' means to persist or continue throughout a period. 'Carry over' means to transfer to the next period. 'Carry along' and 'carry across' are not standard in this figurative sense.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrasal verb means 'to continue or persist throughout a period of time'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'add up' correctly to describe small amounts accumulating?
options: Four minutes a day adds up to more than twenty hours over a year.
Four minutes a day adds up to more than twenty hours over a year, isn't it?
Four minutes a day add up to more than twenty hours over a year.
Four minutes a day adds up more than twenty hours over a year.
mcQuestions.explanation: The subject 'four minutes a day' is treated as a singular quantity, so 'adds up' is correct. 'Isn't it?' is an unnecessary and awkward tag. 'Add up' with a plural verb form is less standard when the subject is a defined quantity. 'Adds up more than' omits the preposition 'to'.
mcQuestions.hint: When a quantity phrase is the subject, the verb is usually singular. Check the preposition after 'add up'.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The sample argues that the real benefit of the habit was not what it looked like on the surface. Have you ever started something for one reason and discovered a different, more important benefit later? What does that suggest about how we decide whether a habit is worth keeping?
